caveats: The user may have run verification outside indexed agent sessions.

evidence_refs.label: No fresh command/test/browser proof found in compact history

open_questions: What is the smallest relevant command or browser task for this diff?
suggested_checks: Attach a fresh command, test, log, screenshot, or trace before marking the finding fixed.
evidence_candidates.why_it_matters: A review without fresh verification evidence can only prove static plausibility, not that the change works.

evidence_procedure_steps.action: Choose the smallest relevant repo command or browser task, rerun it, and attach the fresh output.
evidence_procedure_steps.artifact: command log, test report, screenshot, trace, or QA run artifact
evidence_procedure_steps.gate: No candidate is marked confirmed or fixed using stale or missing verification.
evidence_procedure_steps.input: Failed, stale, or missing command/test/browser evidence.
evidence_procedure_steps.output: Fresh pass/fail evidence linked to the review.

findings.suggestion: Validate before redirecting: accept only local paths, e.g. `if (typeof next === 'string' && /^\/(?![/\\])/.test(next)) return res.redirect(next);` then fall through to res.redirect('/dashboard'). This rejects absolute URLs, protocol-relative `//evil` and backslash `/\evil` bypasses while allowing legit relative paths.
findings.summary: Line 11 `return res.redirect(next)` sends the user-controlled `next` query param (line 9) straight to res.redirect with no allowlist or same-origin check; the line 10 `if (next)` guard only tests presence. /login?next=https://evil.example issues a 302 to the attacker domain (CWE-601), a phishing/credential-theft vector on the auth route.
findings.title: Open redirect: unvalidated req.query.next passed to res.redirect

findings.suggestion: Constrain `next` to internal destinations before redirecting: reject any value that is an absolute URL or starts with `//`, and prefer validating against an explicit allowlist of known internal paths. E.g. `if (next && next.startsWith('/') && !next.startsWith('//')) return res.redirect(next); return res.redirect('/dashboard');` — or resolve `next` against the site origin with the URL API and confirm the resulting host matches your own before redirecting.
findings.summary: At source.js:9-11, `const next = req.query.next` is used directly in `res.redirect(next)` with no allowlist, same-origin, or relative-path check. A link like https://trusted.com/login?next=https://evil.example redirects the victim off the trusted origin to an attacker-controlled page, enabling credential phishing under cover of the legitimate domain. Note a leading-slash check alone is insufficient because protocol-relative `//evil.example` also escapes the origin.
findings.title: Open redirect: user-controlled `next` passed unvalidated to res.redirect

findings.suggestion: Validate `next` before redirecting: require a single-leading-slash relative path and reject `//` and `/\` prefixes (e.g. `if (typeof next === 'string' && /^\/(?![\\/])/.test(next)) res.redirect(next); else res.redirect('/dashboard');`), or match `next` against an explicit allowlist of permitted destinations. Do not rely on `startsWith('/')` alone.
findings.summary: Line 9 (`const next = req.query.next;`) reads an attacker-controlled value and lines 10-11 (`if (next) { return res.redirect(next); }`) pass it directly to res.redirect with no allowlist, same-origin, or relative-path check. An absolute URL (`/login?next=https://evil.example`) or protocol-relative URL (`//evil.example`) is honored, redirecting authenticated users off the trusted domain — a phishing/credential-theft vector on a login endpoint. The inline comment (lines 6-8) accurately describes this bug, so intent and code agree (no assumption-integrity contradiction); the defect itself is a real, obvious security hole worth reporting.
findings.title: Open redirect: unvalidated `next` query param passed to res.redirect

findings.suggestion: Apply the validation fix, remove/replace the BUG comment, and attach fresh proof: curl the /login route with next=https://evil.example (expect redirect to /dashboard), next=//evil.example (expect no off-origin redirect), and next=/settings (expect /settings). Paste the response Location headers as verification evidence.
findings.summary: The commit adds source.js with the `BUG:` comment (lines 6-8) describing the open redirect but applies no mitigation, and no test/command/run evidence is attached (the rerun_relevant_verification gate is blocked). This is a fake-green / incomplete handoff: the next agent may read the retained BUG comment as accepted rather than open.
findings.title: Change ships a documented BUG with no fix and no verification (incomplete handoff)

nodes.label: Prior commits, decisions, agents, and command evidence

nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

Single 14-line new file that is an intentional benchmark case for an open redirect. Assumption-integrity scope is clean: the file's stated intent (line 1) and the inline BUG comment (lines 6-8) accurately match the implementation, so no misleading claim would steer a future agent wrong. However, the described vulnerability is real in code — lines 9-11 pass an unvalidated user-controlled `next` param straight into res.redirect — an obvious high-severity open redirect, reported as an evident cross-scope defect. No fresh runtime verification was produced, but static dataflow proof is conclusive for this class of bug.

New file introduces a classic open-redirect vulnerability: the `next` query parameter (source.js:9) flows unvalidated into res.redirect (source.js:11). From the Product Safety lens this breaks the login flow's trust boundary — an attacker can craft a link on the trusted domain that bounces users to a phishing clone, so it's a user-facing safety regression, not just a theoretical bug. The fix requires rejecting absolute and protocol-relative URLs (or using a path allowlist); a leading-slash-only check would not close the hole.

Single new 14-line Express file that intentionally contains an unmitigated open redirect (CWE-601) at line 11: req.query.next flows unvalidated into res.redirect. Real, high-severity security defect on the login route. Additionally, from the agent-handoff lens, the change was committed with the descriptive BUG comment intact, no fix, and no verification evidence (blocked verification gate). Fix is small and bounded; verification is three curl calls.
